title: Blender Batch Execution Tools
description:
Description

"Blender Batch Execution Tools" is an add-on designed to streamline your workflow by running a specified Python script across multiple .blend files in a single batch process. Instead of manually opening and editing each file, you can now automate the process, applying the same modifications to an entire directory of .blend files quickly and efficiently.

Key Features


  • Batch Execute Scripts: Automatically run a custom Python script on multiple .blend files without manual intervention.
  • Optional Subfolder Inclusion: Easily process all .blend files located in subfolders.
  • Save Versions Toggle: Optionally set the save version count to 0 to avoid creating backup files.
  • Clean Unused Data: Efficiently remove all unused data blocks to keep your .blend files clean.
  • Pack External Resources: Automatically pack all external files into each .blend, ensuring file completeness and portability.

Real-World Use Cases:

  • Batch Rendering: Automatically render multiple .blend files overnight with uniform settings.
  • Mass Renaming: Instantly rename objects or materials across numerous projects to maintain consistent naming conventions.
  • Uniform Scene Setup: Apply standardized scene settings, materials, or textures to all your .blend files at once.






Installation

  1. Download the ZIP File: After purchase, download the provided .zip file (e.g., batch_script_execution.zip) to your computer.
  2. Install via Preferences:
    • In Blender, go to Edit > Preferences... > Add-ons.
    • Click the Install... button and select the downloaded .zip file.
  3. Enable the Add-on:
    • After installation, search for "Blender Batch Execution Tools" in the add-ons list.
    • Check the box to enable the add-on.


Usage Instructions

  1. Open the Panel:

    • In the 3D View, press N to open the Sidebar.
    • Navigate to the "Scripts" tab to find the "Blender Batch Execution Tools" panel.

  2. Select the Script File:

    • Under "Script Selection," choose the Python file you want to execute on the .blend files.

  3. Choose a Directory:

    • Click "Select Directory" to locate the folder containing your .blend files.
    • If desired, enable "Include Subfolders" to process all .blend files within any nested directories.

  4. Set Optional Preferences:

    • Set Save Versions to 0: Disable backup files.
    • Clean Unused Data Blocks: Remove all unused data blocks.
    • Pack Resources: Include all external resources in the .blend.

  5. Run the Batch Process:

    • Click the "RUN" button to start processing.
    • The add-on will execute the chosen script on each .blend file and automatically save the updated files.

  6. Monitor Progress:

    • Keep an eye on the Blender console or system terminal to view progress logs, including the number of files processed and time taken.

Documentation & Support

  • Documentation & Creator Page: https://blendermarket.com/creators/blender-vfx-master
  • For questions, issues, or feedback, please reach out via the creator page. Your suggestions and requests are always welcome.

Updates & Compatibility

  • Current Version: 2.0.0, tested with Blender 3.6.
  • Future updates will ensure compatibility with new Blender versions and incorporate user feedback for better performance and feature enhancements.

This information helps users quickly understand how to install, configure, and utilize the Blender Batch Execution Tools add-on, making it easier for them to integrate it into their workflow.

Support Blender Foundation

We believe in giving back to the community that makes our work possible. Every month, we dedicate a portion of our plugin's revenue to support the Blender Foundation. Thank you for helping us contribute to the future of open-source creativity.
documentation:
Installation Guide

  1. Download the Add-on
    After purchasing the add-on on the Blender Market, download the .zip file to your computer (e.g., batch_script_execution.zip).

  2. Open Blender Preferences
    In Blender, go to Edit > Preferences... and click the Add-ons tab on the left side.

  3. Install the Add-on
    Click the Install... button at the top of the Preferences window.
    Locate the previously downloaded .zip file on your computer and select it.
    Click Install Add-on.

  4. Enable the Add-on
    Once installed, search for "Batch Script Execution" in the Add-ons list.
    Check the box next to its name to enable it.
    Close the Preferences window.

Your add-on is now installed and ready for use.

Usage Tutorial

  1. Open the Panel
    Switch to the 3D View in Blender. Press N to open the Sidebar.
    Navigate to the Scripts tab where you will find the "Batch Script Execution" panel.

  2. Select the Python Script
    Under the "Script Selection" box, choose the Python file you want to execute on multiple .blend files.
    This could be a script that modifies objects, materials, renders, or any custom logic you’ve created.

  3. Choose a Directory
    Click "Select Directory" to pick the folder containing the .blend files you want to process.

    • If your .blend files are stored within subfolders, enable "Include Subfolders" to process them as well.

  4. Configure Options
    Choose from the available options to tailor your batch process:

    • Set Save Versions to 0: No backup versions (.blend1, .blend2) will be created.
    • Clean Unused Data Blocks: Automatically remove data blocks not used by the scene.
    • Pack Resources: Pack all external files (textures, etc.) into each .blend file.

  5. Run the Batch Process
    Once everything is set, click the "RUN" button.
    The add-on will open each .blend file in the chosen directory (and subdirectories, if enabled), run the selected Python script, and then save the file automatically.

  6. Monitor Progress
    The console or terminal window (if open) will display progress, showing how many files have been processed and how long it took.
    After completion, a message will appear indicating the total number of files processed.

Tips & Best Practices

  • Test on a Single File First: Before batch processing dozens of files, run your script on a single .blend to ensure it works as intended.
  • Backup Important Files: Although you can set save versions to 0, consider keeping backups of important files before running batch operations.
  • Check the Console for Errors: If something doesn’t look right, open the Blender console (Window > Toggle System Console on Windows) to view error messages or print statements from your script.

Following the steps above will help you quickly integrate "Batch Script Execution" into your workflow, saving you time and effort whenever you need to run Python scripts across multiple .blend files.
